title: Decomposing symmetric powers
speaker: Bill Casselman
abstract: One problem raised recently by Langlands in connection with the trace formula was the decomposition of symmetric powers of irreducible representations of $\mathrm{GL}(2)$. There is a classical formula for this. I shall explain a new version of this formula and, with luck, describe computer experiments examining the extent to which the idea will work for other groups.

proof_stages.label: Classical symmetric-power decomposition problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates Langlands' question of decomposing S^m(sigma_k) for irreducible GL(2)-representations and emphasizes that the desired information is asymptotic as m, and secondarily k, tends to infinity.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial encoding of decompositions
proof_stages.summary: The decomposition is encoded by trace polynomials and decomposition polynomials, with the GL(2) weights determining multiplicities even though they do not directly give GL(2)-stable subspaces.

proof_stages.label: Computational discovery of quasi-polynomial patterns
proof_stages.summary: Low-dimensional computations, especially for sigma_3 and then sigma_4 and sigma_6, reveal stabilization from the highest-weight end, periodic corrections, and the vector-partition/quasi-polynomial nature of the coefficients.

proof_stages.label: q-binomial trace formula
proof_stages.summary: A classical q-binomial formula, recalled from Goodman-Wallach and related to q-analogues of factorials and Pascal's triangle, gives an explicit trace polynomial for S^m(sigma_k).

proof_stages.label: Weyl-denominator conversion to decomposition polynomials
proof_stages.summary: Multiplication by 1-q, the GL(2) Weyl denominator, followed by the appropriate truncation converts trace polynomials into decomposition polynomials and explains the stable Taylor-series tails and the sigma_3 finite formula.

proof_stages.label: GL(2) asymptotic distribution and derivative shape
proof_stages.summary: For fixed k and m tending to infinity, the powers q^{m+i} coalesce so that the trace polynomial approaches a convolution/binomial distribution, while applying 1-q turns the limiting profile for decompositions into the corresponding derivative shape, approaching a Gaussian derivative as k grows.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank Weyl-denominator conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The GL(2) mechanism is extrapolated to higher rank by expecting the Weyl character denominator to become an antisymmetric differential operator applied to convolution-type limits built from Weyl-orbit hulls of fundamental representations.

proof_stages.label: Application to Langlands basic functions
proof_stages.summary: The representation-theoretic decomposition problem is connected to the computation of basic functions via the Satake transform, with GSp4 and quasi-split groups presented as current work and evidence for broader applicability.

examples.name: Normal-curve comparison
examples.note: Overlaying the computed trace-polynomial profile with the normal curve is described as the visual observation that revealed the central-limit behavior and suggested why the decompositions look like derivatives.
examples.engagement_reason: He actively compares the plotted data with the normal curve and its derivative, explains the effect of multiplying by 1-q as a derivative after scaling, and later relates the limit to a binomial distribution.
